relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Imitation flows through Paul only as he imitates Messiah.
Tradition is held as received wisdom.
Head/source language frames ordered belonging before the Divine.
Prayer and prophecy carry visible honor.
Women pray and prophesy; Paul’s concern is public honor/order.
Covering language guards against shame within that setting.
Glory language is worked through creation imagery.
Paul recalls woman from man.
He recalls creation for relational purpose.
Authority upon the head remains a mysterious sign before messengers.
In the Lord, woman and man are not apart from each other.
Man is through woman, and all are from the Divine.
The community is asked to discern fitting practice.
Nature/custom imagery is invoked around hair and honor.
Hair becomes glory and covering imagery.
Contentiousness is not to rule the assemblies.
Their gatherings have become worse rather than better.
Splits appear even at assembly.
Factions reveal what is approved and what is fractured.
Their meal no longer truly functions as the Lord’s supper.
Some are hungry while others are drunk.
The poor are shamed at the very meal of the body.
Paul hands over the night of betrayal tradition.
Broken bread becomes body for you in remembrance.
The cup becomes new covenant in blood.
Eating and drinking proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes.
Unworthy participation violates body and blood.
Self-examination prepares the meal.
Judgment comes when the body is not discerned.
Weakness and sickness reveal communal disorder.
Discernment would spare judgment.
Discipline aims to prevent condemnation with the world.
Wait for one another.
Eat at home if hungry; the assembly meal must not become judgment.

contemplative_note: 1 Corinthians 11 is about embodied honor and table justice. Women are praying and prophesying; the Lord’s supper fails when the poor are humiliated and the body is not discerned.
